message:
fuzz: stop mutating the fuzzing process and adapt to API changes

Tighten fuzz-target safety so harnesses cannot break the fuzzer itself
by issuing requests that mutate the current process, and refresh the
ELF-loader call site against the new ElfHashTable::lookup signature.

fuzz_security_syscalls.rs (bd-sec-syscalls):

- Drop the SECCOMP filter-installation and strict-mode paths from the
  documented strategy. seccomp(2) requests beyond GET_ACTION_AVAIL /
  GET_NOTIF_SIZES would alter the live fuzzing process's filter chain
  and have produced flaky / unreproducible coverage.
- Replace the disjunctive seccomp rc assertion (rc == 0 || rc == -1
  || rc > 0) with a single rc >= -1 invariant that is equivalent for
  any libc-shaped return value but easier to reason about.
- Add pick_failing_perf_target((pid_seed, cpu_seed, group_fd_seed))
  that maps the fuzz seed into one of four (-1,-1,-1) /
  (-1, c_int::MAX, -1) / (pid_t::MAX, -1, -1) / (pid_t::MIN, -1, -1)
  tuples, all of which always fail perf_event_open(2) but exercise
  different kernel validation paths (target lookup, cpu-id range,
  pid-id range). Comments rewritten to describe these invariants and
  to remove the now-stale "any seccomp-returned fd is closed" line.

fuzz_ptrace.rs:

- Remove libc::PTRACE_TRACEME from the REQUESTS table and document why
  in the header: PTRACE_TRACEME ignores its pid argument and instead
  enables tracing on the calling process, which would attach a debugger
  to the fuzzer itself. The remaining requests (CONT, SYSCALL, DETACH,
  KILL, SEIZE, PEEKDATA, POKEDATA, PEEKTEXT, PEEKUSER) all honour the
  pid argument and fail safely against the bogus pids the harness uses.

fuzz_vector_io.rs:

- Drop unused `mut` on iov_w / iov_r bindings. iovec_array writes into
  the inner buffer slices via *mut pointers, so the outer Vec<iovec>
  doesn't need a mutable binding.

fuzz_elf_loader.rs:

- Pass the two new tail arguments (`&[]`, `&[]`) to
  ElfHashTable::lookup, matching its updated signature. Empty slices
  preserve the previous coverage for the raw-view fuzz path.
